items.q: Why did Stalin reject the Marshall Plan?
items.correct: He saw it as dollar imperialism that would pull Eastern Europe into the US orbit; he set up Comecon as an Eastern bloc alternative and blocked satellite states from joining.
items.misconception: Stalin rejected the Marshall Plan because the USSR did not need money after WWII.
items.random_wrong: Stalin rejected the Marshall Plan because it required countries to fight Germany again.
items.why: The USSR was devastated by war and desperately needed aid. Stalin's refusal was strategic, not economic — accepting US dollars meant accepting US influence.

items.q: Why did Japan attack Pearl Harbor?
items.correct: To cripple the US Pacific Fleet and buy time to secure resource-rich Southeast Asia after the US oil embargo threatened Japanese expansion.
items.misconception: Japan attacked because it wanted to conquer America.
items.random_wrong: Japan attacked to support Germany's invasion of the USSR.
items.why: Japan never planned to conquer the US — the goal was a defensive perimeter. Students assume the attack signalled invasion ambitions when it was a desperate strategic gamble.

items.q: Why was Jenner's smallpox vaccination significant in 1796?
items.correct: He showed cowpox material could prevent smallpox safely — the first effective vaccine, though he did not know why it worked; germ theory came nearly a century later.
items.misconception: Jenner discovered germs and used them to make vaccines.
items.random_wrong: Jenner used penicillin to cure smallpox.
items.why: Jenner worked empirically, not from germ theory which was Pasteur's. Students collapse the discoveries together; vaccination predates the science explaining it.

items.correct: A roadside inn for merchants and their animals
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A roadside inn for merchants and their animals. “A type of medieval ship” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which town is usually identified as the original leader of the Hanseatic League?
items.why: It's Lübeck. “Bruges” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately in which century was the Hanseatic League formally consolidated?
items.why: Exact answer: 13. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
